schools.summary: Reconstruct the recurring strife from the Synoptic parallels; the comparative μείζων; the disciples' temporal-kingdom expectation and as-yet-unconceded Petrine primacy.
Calvin/Gill: ambition concealed is ambition still; the flesh fastened on glory and forgot the predicted cross.
Jesus heals ambition by an 'emulation in lowliness,' setting the child as pattern of innocence, not of age.
Chrysostom: even the apostles' failings are above ours, and they soon laid this one aside.
Wesley/Clarke: 'which of us shall be prime minister?' — they still dreamed of a temporal kingdom.
Henry: Christ's answer 'disallows and condemns the thing itself,' leaving no room for a Petrine supremacy.

outliers.note: Alexandrian: the child set in the midst may figure the Holy Spirit humbling himself in the disciples' hearts.

schools.summary: The child was 'called,' so could walk; the scene is in Peter's house; Mark adds the embrace and the saying about being last of all.
Calvin/Barnes: the likeness is not at all points (children have faults); the one thing imitated is ignorance of rank and ambition.
Clarke/Wesley repeat the tradition that the child was Ignatius of Antioch.

debates.question: Was the child the future martyr Ignatius of Antioch?

sides.position: yes, by old tradition
a worthless legend resting on a misread title

schools.summary: 'Converted' = simply 'turned' from self-seeking; στραφῆτε + strong double negative: otherwise no entrance at all.
Clarke/Wesley: the turn from a temporal to a spiritual, lowly, wholly dependent mind.
Calvin: they must 'wheel round' from the customs of men; Christ gives no place to one who clings to rank.
Cyril of Jerusalem links it to John 3:5: the new birth of water and Spirit, a twofold cleansing.

debates.question: Does 'be converted' reach beyond the turn from ambition to regeneration/new birth?

sides.position: the narrower turn from self-seeking
also the inward renewal / new birth

schools.summary: Humbling oneself in order to be great would defeat itself; the child is lowly by nature, the disciple must make himself so.
Calvin's definition: claim no merit before God, despise no brother, desire only that the Head be exalted.
Origen measures greatness by advance toward the child's freedom from the passions.
'In my name' = for my sake; the child symbolizes the believer; this is the first utterance of the judgment-law of Matthew 25:40.
Calvin: consolation — Christ protects the lowly and commends them to men's favour.
Wesley: the little ones are dear to Christ; help them as if it were Christ himself.
Origen allegorizes the manhood mortified into childlike freedom from passion.

themes: receiving Christ in his little ones

schools.summary: μύλος ὀνικός = the large ass-turned millstone; πέλαγος = open sea; the drowning was a Greco-Roman, not Jewish, penalty.
Calvin: comfort for the despised godly and threat to the proud who trample the weak; 'offend' = cause to stumble or retard.
Hilary/Gregory allegorize the millstone as the dull round of earthly or vain toil.
Origen: the 'little one' is the newly born in Christ, still capable of being made to stumble.
The inevitability is a kind of moral law rooted in human depravity, yet each offender's will is free and accountable.
Chrysostom: the prophecy does not cause the offences; the lament is itself a healing that moves many to amend.
Calvin: dread offences above all; God leaves his people exposed to sift wheat from chaff, but the guilt is the offender's.
Origen: the offences are Satan's army seeking instruments, on whom falls the heavier woe.
Hilary/Jerome narrow 'that man' to Judas and to the Jews who took offence at the cross.

debates.question: How can offences be 'necessary' and yet the offender guilty?

sides.position: the necessity is moral/foreknown, not coercive; the will stays free
grounded in providence sifting the church (1 Cor 11:19)

schools.summary: A doublet of Matt 5:29–30; first Matthean αἰώνιος; Robertson: same word makes the fire and the life equally unending; Barnes: proof of eternal punishment.
Wesley: even the most beloved, useful person who turns us from the way must be cut off — hard only to flesh and blood.
Origen: the body of the church may sever a stumbling member so the rest enter life.
Barnes simply refers back to v.8: better to enter life one-eyed than be cast whole into hell-fire.
JFB only glance at the law standing behind the passage.

Origen canvasses three theories of the angel; Gregory (via Dionysius) sets them in the lesser ranks yet never leaving God's vision; Augustine: 'ours' as fellow-citizens, the 'face' = self-manifestation.
Clarke/Ellicott affirm individual guardian angels of the Presence; Robertson notes Jewish national angels and leaves it open, stressing the Father's special care.
Calvin denies the one-angel-per-person notion: many angels guard the godly (Ps 34:7); dismisses the good/evil-angel fancy.
Cyril of Jerusalem: even angels behold God only as far as they can bear, not as he is.

debates.question: Does each believer have a single personal guardian angel?

sides.position: yes, a personal guardian of the Presence
no; many angels guard the godly, not one each
undecided, but the Father surely cares

schools.summary: Note the verse is absent from many best MSS.; if genuine, the angels share the Son's saving work and the child's only claim is his need.
Calvin: Christ came to save even the dead; esteem the little ones for Christ's sake, not their virtues.
Wesley/Clarke: the strongest reason not to despise them — Christ came to seek and save the lost.
Cyril of Jerusalem: the Son willingly went to the cross lest the world perish in its sins.

debates.question: Is Matthew 18:11 a genuine part of the text?

sides.position: likely absent / textually doubtful
authentic, secured by the Luke 19:10 parallel

schools.summary: Prefer 'leave the ninety-nine upon the mountains'; refer to the fuller Luke 15 form; the 99 = unfallen, or the relatively blameless.
Calvin distinguishes Matthew's aim (recover the wandering disciple) from Luke's (the whole race belongs to God; joy over one restored).
Origen defers to his Homilies on Luke rather than expounding the verse here.
Clarke/Ellicott: the natural joy over an unexpected recovery; a grammatical refinement ('over it').
Gill: finding is uncertain for the shepherd but certain for Christ, who finds all he seeks.
Cyril of Jerusalem digresses on how eternal life is gained — by faith and by the gospel.
'It is not the will' is rhetorically the opposite affirmation; Ellicott links it to 1 Tim 2:4; Robertson weighs the 'your/my Father' variant.
Clarke: if any perish it is not for want of God's will or provision but their own preference of darkness.
Gill keeps it within the Father's sovereign, securing will toward the little ones.

debates.question: Is the 'will' here the universal saving will or the effectual will securing the elect?

sides.position: universal saving will, resistible by man
sovereign/effectual will securing the little ones

schools.summary: Chrysostom: a new method bringing the wronged to the wrongdoer; 'tell his fault' as a physician, for correction not revenge, in private.
Augustine: rebuke in love, not to wound; rebuking a private sin publicly betrays rather than corrects.
Lightfoot grounds it in Lev 19:17 and the Talmud, but Christ asks a higher charity from the offended party.
Gill/Calvin restrict the rule to brethren and church members; 'gained thy brother' = the winning of a soul.
Henry: the rule preserves both the peace and the purity of the church.

debates.question: Does the rule cover all sins or only lesser ones?

sides.position: all sins, by Christ's broad philanthropy
only lesser sins; grave sinners are scarcely 'brethren' (1 Cor 5:11)

schools.summary: Rooted in Deut 19:15; the one or two are to persuade and to serve as disinterested witnesses that every effort was made.
Calvin: the step assumes obstinacy, not flat denial; one who denies the fact must be left alone, ἔλεγξον = convince by demonstration.
Ellicott: ἐκκλησία = 'congregation,' the appeal to the society at large, not its rulers; Robertson: the local body; 'heathen/publican' = cease communion, not kindness.
Calvin: Christ borrowed the Sanhedrin's existing excommunication; gospel discipline succeeds the ancient form.
Jerome sharpens it: a believer living as an unbeliever is more to be abhorred than open Gentiles.

sides.position: the whole congregation/assembly, not its rulers
the local body or its appointed representatives
the apostles as the church then in being

schools.summary: Jerome/Hilary/Gloss: the apostolic sentence of excommunication and absolution is ratified in heaven; Augustine: only if bound with just cause.
Clarke/Lightfoot: rabbinic 'bind/loose' = declare unlawful/lawful, a declaratory power over things (neuter); Barnes restricts the promise to the apostles.
Calvin: ratifies only what proceeds from Christ's word and Spirit; the passage gives Rome no warrant for tyranny.
Chrysostom: 'if ye shall bind,' left to discretion; the incorrigible bound by present and future punishment.

debates.question: Does 'bind and loose' mean church discipline or declaring lawful/unlawful?

sides.position: discipline — excommunication and absolution
declaratory teaching authority over things (rabbinic sense)
both, condemning/absolving by interpreting the Law

schools.summary: Barnes restricts it to the apostles ordering the church; συμφωνέω = 'symphony,' concord of hearts; Ellicott guards it with the conditions of v.20.
Calvin: a remarkable promise to public prayer, joining God's word with united supplication.
Henry: applies broadly to all the faithful and particularly to prayer for binding/loosing — prayer must accompany discipline.
Origen hears in the 'agreement' the harmony of the two covenants joined by the Spirit.

debates.question: How broad is this promise of answered prayer?

sides.position: narrow — the apostles ordering the church, not ordinary prayer
broad — all united/public prayer of the faithful

schools.summary: Barnes/Clarke: the saying proves Christ's omnipresence and deity; 'in my name' = gathered as disciples by his authority; strength measured by conditions, not numbers.
Calvin: a wider promise of Christ's guiding presence; spur to assemble, warning against ungodly councils that claim the name falsely.
Chrysostom: Christ as chief motive of love is present; explains why some agreed prayers go unheard (inexpedient, vengeful, for the unrepentant).
Origen: 'there am I' — present at the very moment of concord, by the Spirit.
The rabbis pardoned thrice, not the fourth; Peter doubled it and thought himself generous; one who asks does not know what forgiveness means.
Jerome/Augustine: 'seventy times seven' is limitless; forgive as God in Christ forgave you (Col 3:13).
Calvin: Peter speaks from the flesh; Christ sets no limit; distinguish goodwill to the impenitent from reconciliation of the penitent.
Origen rejects a wooden arithmetic of forgiveness as unworthy of Peter and of Jesus.
The idiom (77 or 490) is ambiguous but means unlimited forgiveness; a deliberate reversal of Lamech's vengeance (Gen 4:24).
Chrysostom: not a definite number but the endless and ever-enduring.
Wesley: as often as there is occasion; a certain number put for an uncertain.
The 'servants' are petty kings/revenue-farmers; the reckoning is the day of account (Rom 14:12); συναῖραι λόγον in the papyri.
Calvin: three contrasts (king/servant, vast/small sum, kindness/cruelty); cruelty to a brother judges ill for ourselves.
Augustine/Raban: the talents = all sins under the Decalogue; Remigius: the king is Father and Son, the servant all mankind; Chrysostom: the king's unspeakable tenderness.

outliers.note: Refers the reckoning to the consummation and reads the King Christologically as absolute Wisdom, Righteousness, and Kingdom.

schools.summary: Compute the vast sum (millions/highest Greek number); it figures the impossibility of ever clearing the aggregate of sin; the debtor is a tributary prince.
Wesley: the vast weight of our offences and our utter incapacity to satisfy God.
Origen: the reckoning begins at the house of God (1 Pet 4:17), 'like the twinkling of an eye.'

themes: immensity of our debt to God

schools.summary: Grounded in the law of selling debtors (2 Kgs 4:1; Lev 25:39; Ex 22:3); Ellicott warns against allegorizing the legal framework.
Gill: every sinner an insolvent bankrupt; sin against an infinite God is an infinite debt only Christ can pay; Calvin: prayer the sole refuge.
Origen reads the selling redemptively — sold to another, the man might yet prosper and pay all.
The 'worship' is the prostrate homage before an eastern king; 'I will pay all' is the convicted sinner's vain impulse to be justified by works.
Clarke: a pattern of penitence; the sinner 'pays all' only by pleading Christ's sacrifice by faith.
Gill: the vow is empty; the man owns his debt and present inability, his promise resting on no real ability.
The forgiveness flows from God's eternal mercy alone; Ellicott: met with more than asked — the man is now 'justified by faith'; the 'debt' is a loan, life lent to us.
Gill: moved not by the servant's words, deeds, or deserts but by pure mercy and free grace.
The hundred denarii are small beside the talents (Barnes), yet a real sum (Ellicott) — sins against brothers can be grievous, trifling only beside sins against God; ἐπνιγεν = 'began to throttle.'
Ellicott: a pardoned man turns merciless because he is 'double minded' (Jas 1:8), justified but not yet sanctified.
The fellow-servant pleads in the very words of v.26; Ellicott: this debt could really be paid — man can atone toward man, not toward God; Clarke notes textual variants.
Gill: he begged just as the first servant had at his Lord's feet.
Imperfect ἤθελεν = persistent refusal; the imprisonment is suicidal, cutting off the labour by which the debt could be paid.
Clarke: to the unmerciful God shows no mercy; he teaches us how to treat others by how he treats us.
Barnes: a mere narrative circumstance — do not over-allegorize; Ellicott: the fellow-servants' mood is sorrow, not anger, the mark of the true disciple.
Calvin: no mystery, only what nature teaches — men become witnesses against the cruel; severe creditors are unworthy of mercy.
Remigius allegorizes the fellow-servants as angels or faithful preachers grieving a brother's perdition.
Clarke: the servant stands confounded and speechless; Ellicott: 'entreatedst me' — he was forgiven above all he asked.
Robertson: the king fits the cap on the wicked slave; Ellicott links it to 'forgive us… as we forgive' (Matt 6:12).
Clarke: it became thee to show mercy, being so deep in debt thyself and having obtained mercy.
Gill: those who have received mercy should show mercy.
Barnes: 'tormentors' = jailers, imprisonment not torture; Robertson: punitive not purgatorial — the debt could never be paid; Ellicott: vague symbols of God's retribution.
Calvin: the 'till' gives no warrant for purgatory; the punishment is eternal, the debt unpayable.
Wesley: a warning that a pardon once granted can be retracted — the forgiven debt was required again.

debates.question: Does 'till he should pay all' teach purgatory, eternal punishment, or a retractable pardon?

sides.position: eternal, punitive — not purgatory (debt unpayable)
a retracted pardon and unending torment
deliberately vague symbols of retribution / conscience

schools.summary: 'From your hearts' = no sham or lip pardon, but real feeling and action; Christ's full answer to Peter; the closing words textually doubtful.
Ellicott: the verse cuts through false security — 'the debt may come back'; if faith does not work by love it ceases to justify.
Gill: God acts in kind toward the merciless and unforgiving spirit.
